message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>
                    B
                    <jats:sc>ackground</jats:sc>
                  </jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The effective reproductive number
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>e</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    (
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                    ) is a critical measure of epidemic potential.
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>e</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    (
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                    ) can be calculated in near real time using an incidence time series and the generation time distribution—the time between infection events in an infector-infectee pair. In calculating
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>e</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    (
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                    ), the generation time distribution is often approximated by the serial interval distribution—the time between symptom onset in an infector-infectee pair. However, while generation time must be positive by definition, serial interval can be negative if transmission can occur before symptoms, such as in
                    <jats:sc>covid</jats:sc>
                    -19, rendering such an approximation improper in some contexts.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>
                    M
                    <jats:sc>ethods</jats:sc>
                  </jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We developed a method to infer the generation time distribution from parametric definitions of the serial interval and incubation period distributions. We then compared estimates of
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>e</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    (
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                    ) for
                    <jats:sc>covid</jats:sc>
                    -19 in the Greater Toronto Area of Canada using: negative-permitting versus non-negative serial interval distributions, versus the inferred generation time distribution.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>
                    R
                    <jats:sc>esults</jats:sc>
                  </jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We estimated the generation time of
                    <jats:sc>covid</jats:sc>
                    -19 to be Gamma-distributed with mean 3.99 and standard deviation 2.96 days. Relative to the generation time distribution, non-negative serial interval distribution caused overestimation of
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>e</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    (
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                    ) due to larger mean, while negative-permitting serial interval distribution caused underestimation of
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>e</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    (
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                    ) due to larger variance.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>
                    I
                    <jats:sc>mplications</jats:sc>
                  </jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Approximation of the generation time distribution of
                    <jats:sc>covid</jats:sc>
                    -19 with non-negative or negative-permitting serial interval distributions when calculating
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>e</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    (
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                    ) may result in over or underestimation of transmission potential, respectively.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Estimating effective reproduction number using generation time versus serial interval, with application to COVID-19 in the Greater Toronto Area, Canada

reference.unstructured: Robert V Hogg , Joseph McKean , and Allen T Craig . Introduction to mathematical statistics. Pearson Education, 2005.
